title: Farmers Face High Costs for Growing Food
summary: Farmers in the UK are having a hard time because the prices of fertiliser and fuel are going up a lot. Fertiliser helps plants grow, and fuel is used for farm machines. A farmer named Andrew Williamson says it is very expensive now, and this makes it tough for farmers to make enough money. He grows crops like wheat and barley on his farm. The high costs come after two bad harvests, which means farmers already had less food to sell. Livestock farmers, who raise animals, are also affected because they buy fertiliser when they need it, not ahead of time. This situation makes farming risky because farmers have to wait a long time to see if their crops will sell well. The article explains why this is a big problem for farmers and how it might make food more expensive for everyone.

keywords.explanation: a substance that helps plants grow better
energy used to run machines like tractors
people who grow crops or raise animals for food
plants like wheat or barley that are grown for food
the time when crops are collected from the fields

questions.question: What are two things that are getting more expensive for farmers?
Who is the farmer mentioned in the article?
What does fertiliser help with on a farm?
Why are livestock farmers especially affected by the price rise?
options: They buy fertiliser when they need it
questions.correct_answer: They buy fertiliser when they need it

questions.question: What happened before the high costs that made things worse for farmers?
What is one risk farmers face because of the high costs?
options: It is harder to make money
questions.correct_answer: It is harder to make money

background_read: Farming is an important job where people grow food like wheat, barley, and oats, or raise animals. Farmers use fertiliser to help their crops grow strong and fuel to power machines like tractors. Sometimes, prices for these things go up, which can make it difficult for farmers to afford what they need. This article talks about how rising costs are affecting farmers in the UK, making it risky for them to plan for the future and possibly leading to higher food prices for everyone.
Article_Structure: WHO: Farmers like Andrew Williamson in the UK
WHAT: Struggling with high prices of fertiliser and fuel
WHERE: On farms across the UK, such as in Shropshire
WHY: Because rising costs make it hard to grow crops and make money, especially after bad harvests

perspectives.description: Farmers are worried and frustrated because high costs make farming risky and less profitable.
